title: Offices of Physical, Occupational and Speech Therapists, and Audiologists

narrative.executive_view: This is a durable-demand, modest-AI-capture thesis rather than a clinical-labor replacement thesis. NAICS 621340 covers independent PT, OT, speech and audiology practices, including practices operating in another facility, while skilled nursing delivered in the home is outside this code [S1]. The investable center is multi-site outpatient rehabilitation. AI can remove a meaningful share of scheduling, eligibility, coding, denial-management and documentation work, but licensed therapist time is usually the paid service. A disciplined buyer can capture back-office savings and throughput, yet recurring payer pressure and weak exclusivity limit retention. Buy at about 6.5x normalized EBITDA and underwrite a 10.0x scaled-platform exit only as a bounded estimate supported by current lower-middle-market and platform ranges [S8][S9], not as guaranteed expansion.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The clean replacement surface is administrative: intake, scheduling, insurance verification, prior authorization, claims follow-up, coding assistance and routine patient communication. AI scribes can also draft notes and compliance checks, but in the licensed roles this primarily returns unbilled time or creates visit capacity rather than eliminating the therapist who performs the billable intervention. WebPT's 2025 industry report identifies documentation, billing, compliance and front-desk workflows as the main AI opportunities [S4]. Digital MSK platforms are a separate substitution channel: Hinge describes AI-guided exercise delivered with licensed clinicians and says digital MSK remains early, so low-acuity exercise episodes face pressure while manual therapy, complex assessment and relationship-intensive care remain human [S5].
narrative.value_capture: Capture is mixed. Medicare therapy remains fee-schedule based, assistant services carrying CQ/CO modifiers are paid at 85% of the otherwise applicable amount, and multiple-procedure payment reduction applies to later same-day therapy services [S2]. The 2026 physician fee schedule raised conversion factors partly through a one-year statutory increase while also applying an efficiency adjustment to certain non-time-based services [S3]; that illustrates annual rate-setting rather than a permanent technology rent. Operators can retain direct overhead savings and use reclaimed documentation time for more visits where demand and therapist capacity allow, but payer contracting, utilization controls and referral concentration share the benefit. Clinic reputation and referral relationships create some local moat, not durable pricing power.
narrative.adoption_timing: Adoption is underway but not yet pervasive enough to call 50% effective use across the relevant workflows. WebPT reports increasing interest and concrete use cases, while Hinge characterizes the broader digital MSK market as early [S4][S5]. I estimate current effective adoption at 20% and four years to 50%, with wide ranges because vendor surveys measure interest or use of particular tools rather than verified, workflow-weighted labor displacement. Integration, HIPAA review, clinician trust and uneven small-practice systems slow deployment; packaged EMR-integrated scribes, RCM tools and scheduling automation accelerate it.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The target universe is large in the supplied dataset, but the relevant 821-firm EBITDA-band estimate spans 100-499 and 500-plus employee classes and multiple therapy specialties. Current evidence shows several active consolidators: USPH continued buying multi-clinic practices in 2025 [S6], Arete launched with two acquisitions [S12], and PT Solutions acquired OrthoCarolina's therapy branch [S13]; Provident's market update also records ongoing sector transactions [S8]. USPH's partnership model typically leaves founders with material equity and buys remaining interests at the same EBITDA multiple as the original purchase [S7], highlighting both retention alignment and limited automatic multiple arbitrage. I use four visibly active consolidators, a 6.5x buy and 10.0x exit, with owner-comp normalization and referral concentration as core diligence items.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal paid demand is durable. The Census scope is independent rehabilitation and audiology practice, not a generic wellness application [S1]. AI cannot perform hands-on examination, manual treatment or the accountable licensed encounter; even Hinge's technology-led model retains licensed physical therapists and launched an in-person provider network [S5]. Digital MSK can divert uncomplicated exercise-led episodes, and payers can reduce authorized visits, so some volume is pressured. The base case nevertheless retains paid human care for post-surgical, neurologic, pediatric, balance, complex musculoskeletal, speech, occupational and hearing needs. This supports DURABLE terminal demand while keeping capture and substitution risk separate.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are adoption speed, how much documentation time converts into removable labor rather than capacity, and valuation transfer from a clinic group to a scaled platform. The code is heterogeneous: outpatient orthopedic PT, pediatric speech, hand therapy, recreational therapy and audiology have different billing and technology exposure [S1]. OEWS role weights are at 62134 rather than the exact six-digit code, and labor share combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts. Multiples are especially uncertain: public USPH traded at 17.6x in Provident's Q4 2024 snapshot [S8], whereas the current advisory range for lower-middle-market PT groups is 5.5x-8.0x and a scaled platform 10.0x-14.0x [S9]. The selected 6.5x/10.0x pair deliberately sits inside, not above, those ranges.

sources.what_it_supports: Exact 621340 scope: independent PT, OT, speech and audiology practices; examples, practice-setting boundary, and cross-reference of skilled home nursing to 621610.

sources.what_it_supports: Digital MSK substitution channel, AI-guided exercise with licensed clinicians, early-market characterization and the 2025 launch of an in-person provider network.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621340 ($25,528,793,330) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621340 ($45,314,241,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $97,549 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621340); class <5: 19,498 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,169 firms, avg revenue/firm $664,181 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,941 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,624 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,193,863 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $188,630 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,575 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,111,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $491,574 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 460 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,045,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,061,158 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 361 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,374,413 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,433,157 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 821
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Licensed treatment remains the paid service; replacement concentrates in administrative roles, aides, management reporting and the administrative/documentation portions of clinical roles. The weighted role estimate is about 13.5% of wages.
caveats: Therapist documentation time may convert to capacity or reduced burnout rather than headcount removal.
The 24.53% residual mixes billing, audiology, clinical-adjacent and other roles; its fraction is necessarily broad.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: AI can draft notes and plans, but PT examination and hands-on billable treatment persist.
Assistant treatment is human-delivered and reimbursement-linked; only minor paperwork is replaceable.
Speech treatment and assessment remain clinician-led; documentation and simple exercise support are the replaceable slice.
OT assessment and intervention persist; routine documentation and education drafting can automate.
Self-service check-in, optimized flow and equipment routines can absorb some aide time, not patient safety tasks.
Scheduling, eligibility, authorization paperwork, messaging and document preparation are highly automatable.
Hands-on assistant services persist with only a small administrative slice.
Reporting, RCM oversight and compliance review automate; people leadership and referral relationships remain.
Digital intake, reminders, routing and self-scheduling can replace much routine reception work.
Document handling, data entry, claims support and standard correspondence are strong automation candidates.
The residual includes billing and administrative roles with high exposure but also audiology and clinical-adjacent roles with low exposure.

pi_dist.rationale: A clinic retains direct overhead savings and near-term throughput, but annual fee schedules, utilization controls and the link between therapist time and billable units transfer part of the benefit to payers.
caveats: Payer mix and contract cycle vary materially by clinic; 2026's statutory conversion-factor increase is not evidence of permanent pricing power.

pi_moat.rationale: Clinical reputation, local referral ties and therapist availability create switching friction, but fragmented competition and payer networks constrain price and portability after acquisition.
caveats: Referral relationships may be personal to clinicians and can weaken after a transaction.

t50_years.rationale: Concrete vendor use cases and growing interest support adoption, but effective workflow-weighted deployment remains early and clinical replacement is not the target.
caveats: Available surveys report interest or product use, not verified effective adoption across the 621340 workforce.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: AI documentation and operational tools are commercially available and adoption is increasing, but the evidence does not establish majority effective deployment in independent therapy practices.
caveats: Vendor respondents and digital-MSK customers are unlikely to represent the full six-digit industry.

historical_analogs.value: Rehab EMR/RCM adoption, teletherapy and payer-funded digital MSK
historical_analogs.rationale: Past workflow software diffused through practice systems while digital MSK created a parallel channel; AI should follow both patterns rather than replace all clinic care.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Gradual 3-7 year diffusion with faster uptake in scaled groups
caveats: Teletherapy and digital MSK change delivery channels but remain human-supported, so they are imperfect automation analogs.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A conservative owner-age estimate anchored only to broad Census employer-business owner-age tables; no reopened source provided an exact 621340 statistic.
caveats: Broad employer-business owner-age proxy, not six-digit therapy-practice ownership data.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened industry-specific source documented a succession shortage, so the conservative selection is false.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false; true only with industry-specific evidence
caveats: Absence of documented shortage is not evidence that individual practices lack succession risk.

active_consolidators.rationale: Conservative count of four visibly active platform buyers represented by USPH, Arete, PT Solutions and another active platform/buyer recorded in the Provident transaction update.
caveats: The count is a conservative observable floor, not a complete buyer census, and some transactions span adjacent rehabilitation services.

buy_mult.rationale: 6.5x sits inside the reopened 5.5x-8.0x adjusted-EBITDA range for lower-middle-market PT groups and is consistent with active strategic acquisition evidence.
caveats: Advisory range rather than a complete closed-deal tape; normalize owner-clinician compensation, rollover, earnouts and referral concentration.

exit_mult.rationale: 10.0x is the bottom of the reopened 10.0x-14.0x scaled-platform range and well below the 17.6x public USPH snapshot; it requires real scale, management depth and diversified referrals.
caveats: Public-company valuation is not directly comparable to a private exit, and USPH's same-multiple partner buyouts show that expansion is not automatic.

pricing_structure.value: Licensed visits and timed therapy units under government and commercial fee schedules, plus case rates, school contracts and cash-pay/audiology revenue
pricing_structure.rationale: The scope and CMS mechanics show that human-delivered therapy units dominate the core, with payer-set reimbursement and specialty variation.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Medicare fee schedule, commercial per-visit/case arrangements, Medicaid/school contracts and cash pay
caveats: Payer and specialty mix vary sharply; audiology retail economics are not well represented by a therapy-unit model.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A four-year t50 is consistent with current adoption below 50% and produces a moderate, not immediate, realization profile.

terminal_value.justification: Paid licensed rehabilitation, assessment and hands-on care survive mature AI; digital MSK and payer controls pressure low-acuity volume but do not eliminate the core service.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No proxy is presented as exact industry fact: owner age, adoption, dominant subsegment and valuation bridge are explicitly estimates; official Census scope and CMS payment mechanics are direct.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Annual fee schedules, assistant payment reductions, MPPR and commercial utilization management can pass productivity gains to payers; these pre-existing pressures are not attributed to AI.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization factor; OEWS role mix is 5-digit 62134 rather than exact 621340.
sensitivity: A lower true labor share reduces V proportionally.
A PT-heavy exact six-digit role mix would reduce the estimated replaceable share; a billing-heavy mix would increase it.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Physical therapists are the largest listed wage role, current public and private M&A evidence is deepest for outpatient PT, and the Census definition confirms independent therapy practices while excluding skilled home nursing.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Moves A from about 4.0 to 1.7 and materially changes timing, not terminal demand.
Can erase or enlarge the M factor; platform-scale execution is required.
Moves labor-cost uplift by roughly six percentage points of revenue before capture.

reviewer_note: Exact Census scope was applied. Multiples are selected within reopened 2026 advisory ranges and cross-checked against public USPH evidence; they remain estimates with explicit comparability caveats.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
